translation: Indeed, We sent Noah to his people, [saying], "Warn your people before there comes to them a painful punishment
commentary:
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦٓ أَنْ أَنذِرْ قَوْمَكَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

This surah presents one of the most beautiful short narratives in the Quran, capturing the legacy of Prophet Nuh (AS) from beginning to end in remarkable brevity. Allah ﷻ declares: "Indeed, We sent Nuh to his people, [commanding him]: 'Warn your people before there comes to them a painful punishment.'"

The grammatical structure of this opening ayah reveals something significant about Nuh's (AS) mission. The verse begins in the third person—"We sent Nuh"—but then shifts to the imperative second person: "Warn your people." This could have remained entirely in the third person, stating "We sent Nuh to his people so that he would warn them." Instead, Allah ﷻ chose the command form أَنْ أَنذِرْ.

This shift emphasizes the strength and urgency of the imperative. It suggests that Nuh (AS) didn't simply feel he should warn his people—he felt obligated by divine command to do so. He carried this sense of purpose and obligation from Allah ﷻ constantly throughout his mission.

We see a similar construction in Surah Luqman, where Allah ﷻ says He gave Luqman wisdom أَنِ اشْكُرْ. The command form, rather than a statement like "to be grateful," captures both the wisdom of gratitude and the felt obligation to be grateful. It combines "Yes, you should be grateful, and that's wise" with "I feel obligated to be grateful."

The warning is given مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ—before a painful punishment comes to them, hits them, attacks them. The urgency is palpable from the very beginning of the surah.

translation: He said, "O my people, indeed I am to you a clear warner
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّى لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ

Nuh (AS) responds immediately to his divine commission: "He said, 'O my people, indeed I am to you a clear warner.'"

The phrase يَـٰقَوْمِ ("O my people") establishes the intimate yet authoritative relationship Nuh (AS) has with his audience. He's not a foreigner bringing an alien message—he's one of them, speaking to his own nation. The emphasis إِنِّى لَكُمْ ("indeed I am specifically for you") makes his role personal and direct.

He describes himself as نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ—a clear warner. The clarity is emphasized because there will be no excuse for misunderstanding. His message, his role, and the consequences of rejection are all made unmistakably clear from the outset.

translation: [Saying], 'Worship Allah, fear Him and obey me
commentary:
أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ وَأَطِيعُونِ

This ayah captures the essence of every prophetic mission: "Worship Allah, be conscious of Him, and obey me."

These three imperatives are logically and spiritually connected. First, اعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ—enslave yourselves to Allah ﷻ, recognize that you are servants. This is the fundamental reality of our existence on earth. We're here to serve Him.

Second, وَٱتَّقُوهُ—be cautious of Him, maintain consciousness of Him. Once you recognize you're a slave, you must be mindful not to disobey the Master's commands. A slave who violates the master's orders faces consequences. This taqwa naturally flows from recognizing our servitude.

Third, وَأَطِيعُونِ—and obey me. But how will you know what His commands are? You must follow the prophet sent to guide you. The three elements form a complete chain: recognize your servitude, maintain consciousness of your Lord, and follow the guidance delivered through His messenger.

This trinity—worship, God-consciousness, and obedience to the messenger—summarizes the call of all prophets throughout history.

translation: Allah will forgive you of your sins and delay you for a specified term. Indeed, the time [set by] Allah, when it comes, will not be delayed, if you only knew
commentary:
يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ إِنَّ أَجَلَ ٱللَّهِ إِذَا جَآءَ لَا يُؤَخَّرُ ۖ لَوْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

Nuh (AS) presents the consequences of heeding his call: "He will forgive for you some of your sins and delay you for a specified term. Indeed, the appointed time of Allah, when it comes, will not be delayed—if only you knew."

Notice the wording: يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ—"He will forgive for you from your sins" or "some of your sins." Nuh (AS) doesn't promise complete forgiveness of all sins with certainty. The particle مِن ("from/some") suggests he's hoping for partial forgiveness—perhaps a good number of their sins will be forgiven.

Why this cautious language? Because these words come late in his mission, after centuries of calling his people. His hope has already begun to diminish. He's essentially saying, "I don't know about all your sins, because you've been at this for quite a long time. But perhaps He'll forgive some of your past sins and give you a chance to repent for the rest."

The phrase وَيُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى means He will extend your lives, delay your punishment until a designated deadline. This offers them breathing room, a chance to turn back before it's too late.

But then comes the sobering reality: إِنَّ أَجَلَ ٱللَّهِ إِذَا جَآءَ لَا يُؤَخَّرُ —when Allah's appointed time comes, it cannot be delayed. There won't be a false alarm. There won't be an extension.

The closing phrase لَوْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "if only you knew") is particularly striking. The word لَوْ is what Arabic grammarians call حَرْفُ الْحَسْرَة —the particle of regret. It's typically used to express sorrow over something that has already occurred: "If only..." or "Had you only..."

Yet Nuh (AS) uses it here before the punishment has arrived. Why? Because by this point, the ark is already being built. Every plank he hammers into place is a visible reminder that this is inevitable. People walk by and laugh at him, but he sees the danger is no longer unseen—it's materializing before his eyes. The closer he gets to finishing the ark, the closer divine judgment comes. To him, it's already a done deal. That's why he speaks as though it's already occurred: لَوْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ—"Had you only known."

translation: He said, "My Lord, indeed I invited my people [to truth] night and day
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى دَعَوْتُ قَوْمِى لَيْلًا وَنَهَارًا

Now the narrative shifts. Nuh (AS) turns to complain to his Master, and this is a clear sign he's reached his limit. The way he talks about his people changes. He says: "He said, 'My Lord, indeed I invited my people night and day.'"

The fact that he's now reporting back to Allah ﷻ with this tone of exhaustion signals something significant. Notice the order: لَيْلًا وَنَهَارًا —"night and day." We might expect "day and night," but Nuh (AS) mentions night first. This emphasizes that he lost sleep over this mission. He worked even during hours when no one expected him to—through the night, when most people rest. Night and day, continuously, without pause.

translation: But my invitation increased them not except in flight
commentary:
فَلَمْ يَزِدْهُمْ دُعَآءِىٓ إِلَّا فِرَارًا

"But my call only increased them in flight."

The result of all that tireless effort? فِرَارًا —running away. They didn't just reject him passively; they actively fled from his message. The more he called, the more they ran. Every time he opened his mouth, they became more agitated, more annoyed. Rather than drawing closer, they pulled further away. He saw no progress—only greater aversion. He was socially boycotted, isolated, treated as a pariah.

translation: And indeed, every time I invited them that You may forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted, and were arrogant with [great] arrogance
commentary:
وَإِنِّى كُلَّمَا دَعَوْتُهُمْ لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ جَعَلُوٓا۟ أَصَـٰبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَٱسْتَغْشَوْا۟ ثِيَابَهُمْ وَأَصَرُّوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ ٱسْتِكْبَارًا

Nuh (AS) continues his complaint with devastating detail: "And indeed, every time I invited them that You may forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted, and were arrogant with great arrogance."

The phrase كُلَّمَا means every single time, without exception. And what was his intention? لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ —so that You could forgive them. He wasn't calling them for himself. His concern was entirely for them, for their salvation. This echoes the theme from the previous surah about concern for the other.

But how did they respond? جَعَلُوٓا۟ أَصَـٰبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ —they stuck their fingers in their ears. Picture this: an old man approaches them with a message of life and salvation, and they put their fingers in their ears like children—"La la la, I don't want to hear it!" This is how they treated a messenger of Allah ﷻ.

When a messenger is insulted, how offensive must that be to Allah ﷻ? And for this messenger to be insulted like this for centuries? Nuh (AS) is now recalling it all in his conversation with his Lord.

وَٱسْتَغْشَوْا۟ ثِيَابَهُمْ —they covered themselves with their clothes, pulling fabric over their faces as if to say, "Get away from me, you smell." They treated him with utter contempt and mockery.

وَأَصَرُّوا۟ —and they persisted in their rejection. But the ayah doesn't stop there.

وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ ٱسْتِكْبَارًا —they were arrogant with great arrogance. The verbal noun ٱسْتِكْبَارًا intensifies the meaning: they weren't just arrogant, they were extraordinarily arrogant, condescendingly prideful. Nuh (AS) goes out of his way to detail their crimes before Allah ﷻ.

This passage resonates powerfully with the experience of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. The Quraysh would say about the Quran, "Don't listen to this Quran! Make noise whenever it's being recited so people get distracted!" The parallels are deliberate—Allah ﷻ is teaching His messenger ﷺ about the nature of opposition faced by all prophets.

translation: Then I invited them publicly
commentary:
ثُمَّ إِنِّى دَعَوْتُهُمْ جِهَارًا

"Then indeed I called them publicly."

After private conversations failed, Nuh (AS) changed tactics. If individual approaches led to people pushing him away, he would go to public spaces and call openly. The word جِهَارًا means openly, aloud, in broad daylight where everyone could hear.

translation: Then I announced to them and [also] confided to them secretly
commentary:
ثُمَّ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَنتُ لَهُمْ وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْرَارًا

"Then indeed I announced to them publicly and I confided to them secretly."

Nuh (AS) describes an even more comprehensive strategy. أَعْلَنتُ means he made public announcements—he went to platforms, pulpits, places where formal declarations were made. He figured if private conversations wouldn't work, if speaking on the street wouldn't work, then he would announce it from official venues of communication.

But he didn't stop there. وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْرَارًا —and I spoke to them in secret council. After giving public speeches, he would approach individuals privately: "Did you consider what I was saying? Please, think about what I'm saying to you." The verbal noun إِسْرَارًا emphasizes the depth and repeated nature of these secret counsels.

Nuh (AS) tried every possible approach: individual conversations, public preaching, formal announcements, private follow-ups. He exhausted every avenue of communication.

translation: And said, 'Ask forgiveness of your Lord. Indeed, He is ever a Perpetual Forgiver
commentary:
فَقُلْتُ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفَّارًا

"And I said, 'Ask forgiveness of your Lord. Indeed, He is ever a Perpetual Forgiver.'"

After all those different approaches, what was the core message? ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ—simply ask your Lord to forgive you. That's all. The request is so simple, so accessible.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفَّارًا—He is ever forgiving. The form غَفَّار is an intensive form indicating continuous, repeated forgiveness. He forgives over and over and over again. The verb كَانَ ("He was/has always been") indicates this is His eternal nature.

What follows is a beautiful passage about the worldly benefits of seeking Allah's forgiveness—benefits people don't often associate with istighfar.

translation: He will send [rain from] the sky upon you in [continuing] showers
commentary:
يُرْسِلِ ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْكُم مِّدْرَارًا

"He will send [rain from] the sky upon you in continuing abundance."

The word مِّدْرَارًا describes rain that comes continuously, abundantly, life-giving rain—the kind that's controlled and beneficial, not destructive. This is particularly powerful in retrospect, because the punishment that ultimately destroyed this nation was also water—but water gone wild, uncontrolled, catastrophic. The same element that could have been a blessing became an instrument of destruction.

Rain, when it comes as Allah ﷻ intends, is a source of vegetation, healthy society, flourishing life. That blessing was offered to them through istighfar.

translation: And give you increase in wealth and children and provide for you gardens and provide for you rivers
commentary:
وَيُمْدِدْكُم بِأَمْوَٰلٍ وَبَنِينَ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ أَنْهَـٰرًا

"And give you increase in wealth and children and provide for you gardens and provide for you rivers."

Allah ﷻ will extend you—يُمْدِدْكُم—with wealth and children. These are what people value most in worldly life: financial prosperity and progeny, legacy and security.

But notice something grammatically significant: all these verbs are in the jussive mood. يُرْسِلِ...وَيُمْدِدْكُم...وَيَجْعَل...وَيَجْعَل—each ending with a sukūn, indicating they're all connected to the conditional statement from ayah 10. If you ask forgiveness, then these things will happen: rain will come, wealth will increase, children will be granted, gardens will be installed, rivers will flow.

وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ—He will establish gardens for you. The verb جَعَل suggests installation, putting in place something that will endure.

وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ أَنْهَـٰرًا—and He will establish rivers for you. Life on this earth will become beautiful for you.

This entire passage answers a question we might not have thought to ask: What are the benefits of istighfar? We naturally think the benefit of seeking forgiveness is forgiveness itself. Someone asking for forgiveness hopes primarily for that—to have sins erased. But forgiveness comes with all these additional worldly blessings. Allah's mercy doesn't stop at wiping away sins—it extends to making your life better here and now.

translation: What is [the matter] with you that you do not attribute to Allah [due] grandeur
commentary:
مَّا لَكُمْ لَا تَرْجُونَ لِلَّهِ وَقَارًا

Now Nuh (AS) shifts from promise to question: "What is [the matter] with you that you do not attribute to Allah [due] grandeur?"

مَّا لَكُمْ—what's wrong with you? The phrase suggests bewilderment, almost frustration. لَا تَرْجُونَ لِلَّهِ وَقَارًا—you don't hope for, you don't expect from Allah any dignity, any majesty, any nobility. The word وَقَار encompasses grandeur, dignity, nobility, gravitas.

They don't expect Allah ﷻ to show them honor. They don't anticipate that turning to Him will bring them greatness. This is a devastating spiritual diagnosis—they've lost hope in Allah's generosity and nobility.

translation: While He has created you in stages
commentary:
وَقَدْ خَلَقَكُمْ أَطْوَارًا

"While He has created you in stages?"

وَقَدْ introduces evidence for why they should hope in Allah's nobility. أَطْوَارًا means stages, phases, progressive developments. He's the One who created you stage by stage—you were infants, then children, then adults. You matured, you developed. He's the One who did all that for you.

The argument is clear: If He cared enough to develop you through all these stages, nurturing you from helplessness to capability, why wouldn't you hope in His continued nobility and care?

translation: Do you not consider how Allah has created seven heavens in layers
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَوْا۟ كَيْفَ خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ طِبَاقًا

"Do you not consider how Allah has created seven heavens in layers?"

أَلَمْ تَرَوْا۟—have you not looked, have you not reflected? The verb رَأَى involves both physical sight and intellectual contemplation. كَيْفَ ("how") invites them to examine the manner and sophistication of creation.

سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ طِبَاقًا—seven heavens in layers, one above the other. The word طِبَاق suggests precise layering, perfect stacking. This isn't random—it's architectural, deliberate, magnificent.

translation: And made the moon therein a [reflected] light and made the sun a burning lamp
commentary:
وَجَعَلَ ٱلْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُورًا وَجَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ سِرَاجًا

"And made the moon therein a [reflected] light and made the sun a burning lamp?"

Within those layered heavens, وَجَعَلَ ٱلْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُورًا—He placed the moon as light. The Quran consistently uses نُور (light) for the moon, which reflects light.

وَجَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ سِرَاجًا—and He made the sun a سِرَاج—a lamp, something that generates its own light and heat. Notice the moon is mentioned first, even though the sun is more powerful. Perhaps because the moon is more directly relevant to human timekeeping and monthly cycles, or perhaps to emphasize that even reflected light is a gift.

These cosmic signs are meant to inspire awe and recognition of Allah's power.

translation: And Allah has caused you to grow from the earth a [progressive] growth
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ أَنۢبَتَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ نَبَاتًا

"And Allah has caused you to grow from the earth a [progressive] growth."

وَٱللَّهُ أَنۢبَتَكُم—Allah sprouted you, caused you to grow. The verb أَنْبَتَ is the same used for plants sprouting from the ground. مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ نَبَاتًا—from the earth, a growth.

This connects human origins to the earth in a beautiful parallel. Your physical bodies are made from elements of this earth. Your father Adam (AS) was created from clay. You are earth-creatures, grown from the ground like vegetation, in a sense.

translation: Then He will return you into it and extract you [another] extraction
commentary:
ثُمَّ يُعِيدُكُمْ فِيهَا وَيُخْرِجُكُمْ إِخْرَاجًا

"Then He will return you into it and extract you [another] extraction."

ثُمَّ يُعِيدُكُمْ فِيهَا—then He returns you to it. You came from the earth, and to it you will return when you die. Your bodies will decompose back into the soil.

وَيُخْرِجُكُمْ إِخْرَاجًا—and He will extract you, bring you out again. The verbal noun إِخْرَاجًا emphasizes the completeness of this extraction. This is a clear reference to resurrection—just as He brought you out the first time, He will bring you out again.

The parallel extends: plants sprout, die, drop seeds, and sprout again. Similarly, humans come from earth, return to earth, and will be brought forth again on the Day of Resurrection.

translation: And Allah has made for you the earth an expanse
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ بِسَاطًا

"And Allah has made for you the earth an expanse."

بِسَاطًا means a carpet, something spread out flat, made level and comfortable. Imagine if the earth were all jagged mountains and impossible terrain—you couldn't live, couldn't travel, couldn't build. But Allah ﷻ leveled it down in the places you need, made it walkable, livable, usable.

translation: That you may follow therein roads of passage
commentary:
لِّتَسْلُكُوا۟ مِنْهَا سُبُلًا فِجَاجًا

"That you may travel therein on roads through valleys."

لِّتَسْلُكُوا۟—so that you can traverse, travel through. سُبُلًا—various paths, roads. فِجَاجًا—wide passages, valleys, open spaces between mountains.

The word فَجّ refers to the open space between two mountains—otherwise impassable terrain. Allah ﷻ opened up these pathways for you, made roadways accessible. You can pursue different directions, explore various valleys, reach distant places.

All of these ayahs from 13-20 form an extended argument: Look at what He's done for you. The heavens above, the earth beneath, the celestial bodies, your own growth and resurrection, the pathways opened for you—all of this demonstrates His nobility and care. How can you not hope in Him? How can you not expect dignity from Him?

This is still part of Nuh's (AS) report to Allah ﷻ—"I said this to them, I tried to remind them of all this." He's listing everything he taught them, all the arguments he presented publicly, privately, in formal settings and family settings. He exhausted every avenue.

translation: Noah said, "My Lord, indeed they have disobeyed me and followed him whose wealth and children will not increase him except in loss
commentary:
قَالَ نُوحٌ رَّبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِى وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَن لَّمْ يَزِدْهُ مَالُهُۥ وَوَلَدُهُۥٓ إِلَّا خَسَارًا

Now the tone shifts dramatically. The past tense عَصَوْنِى ("they disobeyed me") indicates finality. He's done with them. 

"Nuh said, 'My Lord, indeed they have disobeyed me and followed one whose wealth and children will not increase him except in loss.'"

إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِى—they have disobeyed me. Not "they are disobeying" but "they have disobeyed." This is a concluded matter in his mind.

وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَن لَّمْ يَزِدْهُ مَالُهُۥ وَوَلَدُهُۥٓ إِلَّا خَسَارًا—and they followed someone whose wealth and children increase him only in loss. They followed leaders who had nothing to offer them except more destruction. These tribal chiefs, these wealthy elite with their children and legacy—all of it amounts to nothing but increasing loss.

The irony is powerful: Nuh (AS) had just promised them, through istighfar, wealth and children as blessings. But they chose to follow people whose wealth and children became curses, sources of greater ruin.

translation: And they conspired an immense conspiracy
commentary:
وَمَكَرُوا۟ مَكْرًا كُبَّارًا

"And they conspired an immense conspiracy."

وَمَكَرُوا۟—they plotted, schemed, conspired. مَكْرًا كُبَّارًا—a huge, immense plot. The form كُبَّار is an intensive plural, suggesting not just something big but enormously significant.

What was this plot? As Nuh (AS) got closer to finishing the ark, his warnings intensified. With only days of work remaining, he became more urgent than ever, more desperately trying to save people. And some were beginning to be shaken—they saw the seriousness in his eyes, the reality of the structure taking shape. They were being pulled toward his message.

The tribal leaders saw this. They saw they were losing control. So they devised a new strategy.

translation: And said, 'Never leave your gods and never leave Wadd or Suwa' or Yaghuth and Ya'uq and Nasr
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَذَرُنَّ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ وَلَا تَذَرُنَّ وَدًّا وَلَا سُوَاعًا وَلَا يَغُوثَ وَيَعُوقَ وَنَسْرًا

"And said, 'Never leave your gods and never leave Wadd or Suwa' or Yaghuth and Ya'uq and Nasr.'"

Their plot was psychological and cultural. لَا تَذَرُنَّ—don't you dare leave, don't you dare abandon. This is emphatic prohibition.

First, ءَالِهَتَكُمْ—your gods in general. Then they name specific idols: وَدّ, سُوَاع, يَغُوث, يَعُوق, and نَسْر.

Why name them individually? To create emotional attachment. They went around conducting a counter-campaign: "You're going to leave Wadd? After everything Wadd has done for you? Have you forgotten his powers? Don't you think a curse will come upon you? You'll be betraying your ancestors! You'll be an embarrassment to your family legacy!"

They made people feel like accepting Nuh's call meant being a traitor—not just abandoning foolish beliefs, but betraying family, tradition, community identity. They weaponized shame, fear of social rejection, and ancestral guilt.

It's not just "don't follow a crazy person"—it's "if you follow him, you're a disgrace to everything we stand for."

And it worked. وَقَدْ أَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا—they misguided many people.

translation: And already they have misled many. And, [my Lord], do not increase the wrongdoers except in error
commentary:
وَقَدْ أَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا ۖ وَلَا تَزِدِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا ضَلَـٰلًا

"And already they have misled many. And [my Lord], do not increase the wrongdoers except in error."

وَقَدْ أَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا—and they have misguided many people. Their campaign was successful. People who might have been on the verge of believing were pulled back.

Now Nuh (AS) makes a stunning supplication: وَلَا تَزِدِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا ضَلَـٰلًا—and don't increase the wrongdoers in anything except more misguidance.

At the very end of his mission, he's trying to save people, and they launch a campaign to mislead Allah's slaves. That's where his anger comes from. He's witnessed them not just reject truth, but actively work to prevent others from reaching it. They're not content to be lost themselves—they want to take as many as possible with them.

So he asks Allah ﷻ: Give them nothing but more deviation. Let them spiral further into their error.

translation: Because of their sins they were drowned and put into the Fire, and they found not for themselves besides Allah [any] helpers
commentary:
مِّمَّا خَطِيٓـَٔـٰتِهِمْ أُغْرِقُوا۟ فَأُدْخِلُوا۟ نَارًا فَلَمْ يَجِدُوا۟ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَنصَارًا

"Because of their sins they were drowned and put into the Fire, and they found not for themselves besides Allah [any] helpers."

مِّمَّا خَطِيٓـَٔـٰتِهِمْ—because of their sins, because of the kinds of mistakes they made. أُغْرِقُوا۟—they were drowned.

Then immediately, فَأُدْخِلُوا۟ نَارًا —they were entered into Fire. 

This nation was tortured with water first, then with fire. The horrors of drowning followed by the horrors of burning. These are two of the most terrifying forms of death, and they experienced both. The element that could have blessed them as abundant rain became the instrument of their destruction in this world, followed immediately by the punishment of fire in the next.

فَلَمْ يَجِدُوا۟ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَنصَارًا —and they found no helpers besides Allah ﷻ. Those idols they refused to abandon? Wadd, Suwa', Yaghuth, Ya'uq, Nasr? Not one of them showed up. Not one could help. No intercessors, no saviors, nothing.

translation: And Noah said, "My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers an inhabitant
commentary:
وَقَالَ نُوحٌ رَّبِّ لَا تَذَرْ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ دَيَّارًا

This is one of the most striking supplications in the Quran. "And Nuh said, 'My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers an inhabitant.'"

لَا تَذَرْ —don't leave. عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ —on the earth. مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ —from among the disbelievers. دَيَّارًا —not a single home-dweller, not a single inhabitant.

How much must they have tested the patience of Nuh (AS) for him to say this? For a prophet—whose whole mission is to guide people, save people—to ask for complete annihilation?

But notice: this is near the end of his career. After 950 years of trying. After exhausting every method. After watching them not only reject guidance but actively mislead others.

translation: Indeed, if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants and not beget except [every] wicked one and [confirmed] disbeliever
commentary:
إِنَّكَ إِن تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا۟ عِبَادَكَ وَلَا يَلِدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا فَاجِرًا كَفَّارًا

He explains his reasoning: "Indeed, if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants and not beget except [every] wicked one and [confirmed] disbeliever."

إِنَّكَ إِن تَذَرْهُمْ —no doubt about it, I know from experience: if You spare any of them, يُضِلُّوا۟ عِبَادَكَ —they will mislead Your slaves.

That's where the anger comes from. He's not just upset about their personal disbelief. He's upset because they're a source of corruption for others. They don't just refuse guidance—they prevent others from accepting it. They're spiritual toxins, poisoning everyone around them.

وَلَا يَلِدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا فَاجِرًا كَفَّارًا —and they won't give birth to anyone except someone extremely sinful and utterly ungrateful. فَاجِر is someone engrossed in sin, impulsively sinful. كَفَّار is the intensive form of kafir—someone extremely ungrateful and deeply entrenched in disbelief.

The corruption has become generational, cyclical. There's no hope for change. They will only produce more like themselves, more sources of misguidance.

This is the assessment of a prophet after nearly a millennium of observation.

translation: My Lord, forgive me and my parents and whoever enters my house a believer and the believing men and believing women. And do not increase the wrongdoers except in destruction
commentary:
رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَىَّ وَلِمَن دَخَلَ بَيْتِىَ مُؤْمِنًا وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَلَا تَزِدِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا تَبَارًۢا

The surah concludes with a beautiful du'a that shifts from wrath back to mercy—but a mercy that's selective, directed toward the believers.

"My Lord, forgive me and my parents and whoever enters my house a believer and the believing men and believing women. And do not increase the wrongdoers except in destruction."

رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ لِى —My Master, forgive me. After everything, after 950 years of service, Nuh (AS) still begins with asking forgiveness for himself. This is the humility of prophethood.

وَلِوَٰلِدَىَّ —and my parents. Despite everything, despite a son who rejected him, he asks for his parents.

وَلِمَن دَخَلَ بَيْتِىَ مُؤْمِنًا —and whoever entered my house as a believer. His home became a sanctuary, a place where the few believers could gather. He asks forgiveness for everyone who sought refuge there with faith.

وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ —and for the believing men and the believing women. He expands his mercy to encompass all believers, male and female, everywhere.

Then the contrast: وَلَا تَزِدِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا تَبَارًۢا —and don't increase the wrongdoers in anything except in destruction.

تَبَار comes from the root meaning to break, to destroy utterly. The only increase he wants for them is in their collapse, their complete annihilation.

What's powerful about this placement is that we're reading Surah Nuh right after Surah Al-Ma'arij. In Al-Ma'arij, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is told: فَٱصْبِرْ صَبْرًا جَمِيلًا —"So be patient with beautiful patience." Just be patient, ignore the ignorant things they say, the torture they inflict, the crimes they commit.

But then immediately after comes Surah Nuh, which teaches: even the most patient human being, after centuries of patience, eventually said, "Don't leave any of them." 

This surah gives Prophet Muhammad ﷺ permission. Allah ﷻ is saying: I'm advising you to be patient, but if you speak out, if you reach your limit, let me tell you what happens when a messenger speaks out. Because you have that right. You have the right to ask for their destruction after what they've done to you.

The mercy shown to the Prophet ﷺ is extraordinary—Nuh (AS) reached his breaking point after 950 years. How long has Muhammad ﷺ been patient? Much, much less. And yet he never made this du'a against his people. He never asked for their complete annihilation, though he had every right to do so.

The placement of these surahs teaches us about patience, about mercy, about the limits of human endurance, and about the superior character of our Prophet ﷺ, who embodied beautiful patience beyond what even Nuh (AS) could maintain.
